channel.title: NYT > Business
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Those British Strawberries Are Being Picked by Central Asian Workers
item.description: Ten years after Brexit, most seasonal workers in Britain are from countries such as Kyrgyzstan and Tajikistan. Without them, agricultural chiefs say, many farms would fail.
item.dc:creator: Stephen Castle and Aigerim Turgunbaeva
item.media:credit: Andrew Testa for The New York Times
item.media:description: Shukrat Djuraev at Homefield Farm, in Kent, England, this month.

item.title: Brexit Has Cost the UK Growth, Analysts Say, in the Decade Since the Vote
item.description: Citing lower trade and investment, analysts broadly agree that Britain’s economy is smaller than it would have been if the country had stayed in the E.U.
item.dc:creator: Eshe Nelson
item.media:credit: Andrew Testa for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ten years ago, British voters approved by a slim margin a proposal for the country to leave the European Union. The economic results, economists say, have not been glowing.

item.title: Tesla Crash That Killed a Texas Woman Will be Investigated by Federal Regulators
item.description: The car’s driver-assistance system was in use when the crash killed a woman on Friday, the police said.
item.dc:creator: Lauren McCarthy
item.media:description: Tesla’s driver-assistance systems have been implicated in a number of accidents, some of them fatal.

item.title: Netflix Buys a ‘Hot Ones’ Spinoff
item.description: The streaming giant bought a spinoff of a popular online interview series that features celebrities eating fiery chicken wings, the latest salvo in its rivalry with YouTube.
item.dc:creator: John Koblin
item.media:description: From left, Will Ferrell, Jimmy Tatro and Fortune Feimster in the new Netflix “Hot Ones: Extra Heat” series.

item.title: In Alan Greenspan, Kevin Warsh Saw a Fed Chairman Role Model
item.description: Kevin M. Warsh has long singled out Alan Greenspan, who died on Monday, as the central banker he wants to exemplify.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith and Ben Casselman
item.media:description: Alan Greenspan at his 1987 confirmation hearing as the nominee for Federal Reserve chairman. He was known for being hard to parse as a leader of the central bank.

item.title: YouTube Stars Take Center Stage at Creative Artists Agency
item.description: For years, creators were on the fringes at Creative Artists Agency, a Hollywood talent behemoth. Now the agency is putting them center stage.
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes
item.media:credit: Michelle Groskopf for The New York Times
item.media:description: Trisha Paytas, a digital star, is part of CAA’s growing roster of creators.

item.title: Clive Davis, Music Industry Titan Who Signed Whitney Houston, Dies at 94
item.description: He rose from a midlevel position at Columbia Records to become one of music’s most powerful executives, shepherding stars like Barry Manilow and Whitney Houston.
item.dc:creator: Ben Sisario
item.media:credit: Bryan Derballa for The New York Times
item.media:description: One of the few executives in music to become a household name, Clive Davis maintained a visible role as a starmaker for more than five decades.

item.title: ‘The View’ Asks Its Audience for Help in Battle With F.C.C.
item.description: The daytime TV talk show is at the center of a dispute with the Federal Communications Commission over political speech.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Mullin
item.media:description: From left, Whoopi Goldberg, Sara Haines, Joy Behar, Ana Navarro, Sunny Hostin and Alyssa Farah Griffin on the set of “The View.”

item.title: U.K. Markets React Calmly to Starmer Resignation, but Economic Uncertainties Linger
item.description: Markets reacted calmly to the prime minister’s announcement, which laid out a timeline for succession. Still, there’s not much clarity about the next government.
item.dc:creator: Eshe Nelson
item.media:description: Keir Starmer, the British prime minister, outside 10 Downing Street on Monday.

item.title: U.S. Oil Is Skipping the Chance to Grab Market Share From the Gulf
item.description: U.S. oil production is expected to grow only modestly next year as companies hesitate to spend more in an uncertain market.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Smaller U.S. oil producers are starting to drill more wells, but big companies like Exxon Mobil and Chevron have said they are not changing their plans as a result of the war with Iran.

item.title: Is China Closing the A.I. Gap Faster Than Expected?
item.description: Silicon Valley and corporate America are increasingly turning to cheaper, open-source artificial intelligence models built in China.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe and Ian Mount
item.media:description: The rapid rise of Chinese A.I. models has impressed many in Silicon Valley, and corporate America.

item.title: China Tightens Rare-Earth Grip on U.S. Firms, Threatening Trade Clash
item.description: The move targets two U.S. manufacturers at the center of the Trump administration’s effort to rebuild the domestic supply chain for critical magnets.
item.dc:creator: Keith Bradsher
item.media:description: MP Materials’ open-pit rare-earth mine in Mountain Pass, Calif., in 2020.

item.title: Oil Prices Fall as U.S.-Iran Talks Show Signs of Progress
item.description: Oil prices retreated after Iran’s foreign minister said there had been “major progress” toward ending the fighting in Lebanon during the first session of high-level talks.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Oil Rises Amid Uncertainty Over Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Modest reactions in the oil and financial markets as Iran and the U.S. met in Switzerland for a first round of talks aimed at making a temporary cease-fire permanent.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Tesla Driver Using Autopilot Crashes Into Home in Texas and Kills a Woman, Officials Say
item.description: The driver told investigators he was using the vehicle’s automated-driver system when the vehicle left the roadway and crashed into a house in Harris County, Texas.
item.dc:creator: Johnny Diaz
item.media:description: The dashboard of a Tesla model using a driver-assistance system in California.

item.title: ‘Toy Story 5’ Fuels Hollywood’s Hottest Summer Since 2019
item.description: The Disney-Pixar sequel was expected to make $160 million in North America over the weekend, lifting the summer box office to $1.85 billion to date.
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes

item.title: While the World Scrambles for Oil, China Sits on Full Tanks
item.description: The possible reopening of the Strait of Hormuz may not prompt China to return quickly to prewar levels of oil purchases from the Persian Gulf.
item.dc:creator: Keith Bradsher
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: An oil refinery in Nanjing, in China’s eastern Jiangsu province, last month. Chinese companies kept their refineries running throughout the war.

item.title: When a Job Goes From Dreamy to Dreary
item.description: Plus, should I follow through on complaints about one workplace after I’ve moved on to another?
item.dc:creator: Max Read
item.media:credit: Photo illustration by Margeaux Walter for The New York Times

item.title: Is Your Vibe ‘High-Signal’ or ‘Anti-Signal’?
item.description: Subtle cues are shaping notions of what’s hot and what’s not in the tech world.
item.dc:creator: Lora Kelley

item.title: Ina Garten to Start a Podcast With Vox Media
item.description: A new show from Ina Garten, a longtime Food Network star, spurred a bidding war, as the lines between video podcasts and talk shows blur.
item.dc:creator: Sapna Maheshwari
item.category: Happy Hour With Ina Garten (Radio Program)
item.media:credit: Lila Barth for The New York Times; Robert Polidori/Edwynn Houk Gallery
item.media:description: Ina Garten, the longtime Food Network Star, will start a new podcast this fall. Instead of cooking, she’ll be making cocktails with her guests.

item.title: The Latest in College Pricing: Tuition at 10 Percent of Your Income
item.description: Whitman College is trying to keep it simple: Tell us your adjusted gross income, and we won’t make you apply and get in just to receive a price quote.
item.dc:creator: Ron Lieber

item.title: How Remote Work Has Helped a Generation of Working Parents
item.description: Post-pandemic, a new openness to accommodating family needs has made it possible for more mothers and fathers to balance work and parenting — particularly mothers of young children.
item.dc:creator: Claire Cain Miller
item.media:credit: Brad Trone for The New York Times
item.media:description: Elizabeth Terhune, with her children in Santa Fe, N.M., worked from home when her youngest was born during the pandemic. Remote work has enabled more mothers of young children to stay in the labor force.

item.title: Mines, Logistics and Deep Uncertainty Threaten a Middle East Oil Rebound
item.description: More oil is getting out of the Persian Gulf, but the region’s producers are looking for signs that it is safe as they ramp up plans for alternative routes.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman and Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Vessels, including oil tankers, anchored this week in the waters off Muscat, Oman, near the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: Egg Prices Are Way Down, but That’s Hurting Farmers
item.description: An oversupply of hens has lowered wholesale egg prices. But consumers may not reap the full benefits because of producer contracts and higher costs.
item.dc:creator: Julie Creswell
item.media:description: Since an avian flu outbreak drove egg prices sky high, wholesale egg prices have plunged 93 percent.

item.title: The Giant Test Kitchen Where Cooks Battle A.I. Slop
item.description: People Inc., the home of Food & Wine and Southern Living, publishes more food content than anyone else. It’s pushing back against the bots with recipes from its culinary hub.
item.dc:creator: Katie Robertson and Bob Miller

item.title: Cottage Cheese Shortages Are Driven by TikTok and ‘Protein-Maxxing’
item.description: TikTok and “protein-maxxing” are driving a cottage cheese squeeze that’s left some consumers desperate.
item.dc:creator: Niko Gallogly and Ahmed Gaber

item.title: A Humble 3-Wheel Electric Vehicle Lands Toyota in Federal Court
item.description: A lawsuit filed in California claims the automaker’s philanthropic arm stole technology intended to help poor farmers, but it is not clear to what end.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing
item.media:credit: Cynthia R. Matonhodze for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mobility for Africa developed a three-wheeled electric vehicle that rural residents of Zimbabwe use to transport produce and other goods to market.

item.title: For New Car Brands, the Path to Success Is Littered With Flops
item.description: Honda and Sony have bailed on Afeela, their joint E.V. project, without selling a single car. Subbrands from big automakers have a shaky track record, but leaning into luxury can help.
item.dc:creator: Eric A. Taub
item.category: Saturn Division of General Motors Corp
Scion Division of Toyota Motor Corp
Lexus Division of Toyota Motor Corp
item.media:description: The Afeela during the CES event this January in Las Vegas. “The name Afeela sounds like a tech company, and it turned some people off,” an advertising expert said.

item.title: Amazon’s Movie Arm Abandons Film About OpenAI
item.description: The company, which invested $50 billion in the artificial intelligence start-up this year, will let the team behind the film, “Artificial,” try to sell the project to another studio.
item.dc:creator: Nicole Sperling
item.media:description: Sam Altman, the chief executive of OpenAI, is the focus of the film.

item.title: For India, the Return of Iranian Oil Cannot Come Soon Enough
item.description: After months of shortages and uncertainty, India sees an opportunity to restore a once-crucial energy relationship.
item.dc:creator: Alex Travelli
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: The Mangalore Refinery in India. As recently as 2009, Iranian oil accounted for 14 percent of India’s crude imports, making Iran its second-largest supplier.

item.title: Rates on New Student Loans Will Rise on July 1
item.description: Higher interest rates take effect on July 1 for the next school year. Borrowers are already dealing with rising college costs and inflation elsewhere.
item.dc:creator: Ann Carrns

item.title: Oil Prices Rise as U.S.-Iran Deal Faces Tests
item.description: Oil remains on track for a second straight weekly decline, but signs of cracks in the agreement to end the war have given traders pause.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Stranded Ships Hoping for Escape Find a Turbulent Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Shipping companies had hoped to get their vessels out after this week’s U.S.-Iran deal but faced uncertainty on Friday as violence flared again in the region.
item.dc:creator: Jenny Gross
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Vessels at the Strait of Hormuz, as seen from Musandam, Oman, on Thursday.

item.title: How Deal With U.S. Could Reconnect Iran to the Global Economy
item.description: The country has long faced some of the world’s heaviest sanctions. The lifting of restrictions could open new vistas on trade and financial access.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Cohen
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: Street vendors in Tehran this week. Years of sanctions and economic mismanagement in Iran have driven inflation and unemployment higher.

item.title: Congress Is Anxious as U.S.-Canada-Mexico Trade Talks Intensify
item.description: With midterms approaching and negotiations heating up, some lawmakers are apprehensive over where the discussions are headed.
item.dc:creator: Adam Sella and Ana Swanson
item.media:description: President Trump’s uncertainty about whether he will renew the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement is causing apprehension in Congress.

item.title: How Much Is SpaceX Really Worth?
item.description: The company’s deep commitment to Mars colonization and Elon Musk’s nearly total control make SpaceX stock an astonishingly speculative bet, our columnist says.
item.dc:creator: Jeff Sommer

item.title: Colby College Is Stepping In to Save a Maine Town After Disaster
item.description: A new rescuer — Colby College, bankrolled by a construction magnate — will vastly change not just the economics of Port Clyde, Maine, but also its cultural and physical fabric.
item.dc:creator: Ralph Blumenthal
item.media:credit: Stacey Cramp for The New York Times

item.title: A Viral Knicks Moment, Brought to You by a Prediction Market
item.description: What do a Jalen Brunson look-alike contest, a buzzy catchphrase and a dancing robot outside Madison Square Garden have in common?
item.dc:creator: Troy Closson, Santul Nerkar and Lauren McCarthy
item.media:description: Jake Epstein, a content creator, hosted a Jalen Brunson look-alike contest for Polymarket, a prediction market company, on Wednesday in Washington Square Park.

item.title: Have You Bought a Tesla Recently? We Want to Hear From You.
item.description: Tesla sales are rebounding in Europe even though many people have negative feelings about Elon Musk, the company’s chief executive. We want to know why.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing
item.media:description: Tesla’s car sales took a big hit in Europe last year, in part because many people were turned off by the political views of the company’s chief executive, Elon Musk.

item.title: The Washington Outlet NOTUS Won’t Be ‘The Star’ After All
item.description: The publication is abandoning a planned rebranding after resolving a trademark dispute with the new publisher of The Washington Star.
item.dc:creator: Katie Robertson
item.media:description: Readers of The Washington Star-News, a previous name of The Washington Star, in 1974.

item.title: Are You a Recent College Graduate Who Moved Back Home? We Want to Hear From You.
item.description: If you lived with your parents for nine months or more after graduation, please tell us how you came to the decision and whether the job market influenced your thinking.
item.dc:creator: Noam Scheiber
item.media:credit: David L. Ryan/The Boston Globe, via Getty Images

item.title: Federal Energy Regulator Seeks to Limit Conflicts Over Data Centers
item.description: The Federal Energy Regulatory Commission directed grid managers to make changes that protect individuals from higher electricity bills while giving data centers access to power faster.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Penn
item.media:credit: Dustin Chambers for The New York Times
item.media:description: A Meta data center in Georgia. U.S. technology companies in recent years have been building dozens of power-hungry data centers to develop artificial intelligence.

item.title: Penske Media Is Buying What’s Left of Vox Media
item.description: The deal will put online publications like Popsugar and Eater under the same roof as Rolling Stone and Variety.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Mullin
item.media:credit: Matt Winkelmeyer/GA, via The Hollywood Reporter, via Getty Images
item.media:description: The Vox Media sites are latest acquisition by Jay Penske, the youngest son of the automotive tycoon Roger Penske.

item.title: Education Department Lowers Student Loan Interest Rates for Two Years
item.description: The department, citing high defaults, is reducing interest rates by up to one percentage point for two years if borrowers pay automatically.
item.dc:creator: Ron Lieber, Tara Siegel Bernard and Michael C. Bender
item.media:description: The temporary rate reduction starts July 1, and borrowers must enroll in automatic payment to obtain it.

item.title: Warsh Wants the Fed to Send Fewer Signals. That Comes With Risks.
item.description: Investors piled on bets for higher borrowing costs after Kevin Warsh opted against providing policy guidance at his first meeting as Federal Reserve chairman.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kevin M. Warsh, the Federal Reserve chairman, believes central bank officials should be more guarded in what they share.

item.title: Warsh’s Hawkish Turn Has Scrambled the Math on Rates
item.description: The futures market now sees at least one interest rate increase this year, as the Federal Reserve’s new leader, Kevin Warsh, vows to fight inflation.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe, Ian Mount and Vivienne Walt
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kevin Warsh, the Fed chairman, surprised many on Wall Street with hawkish statements about the need to combat inflation.

item.title: Inflation Will Linger Despite U.S.-Iran Deal, British Officials Warn
item.description: Bank of England policymakers kept interest rates on hold, saying the impact of the war in Iran was still a major source of uncertainty for the economy.
item.dc:creator: Eshe Nelson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Joseph Horton for The New York Times
item.media:description: Policymakers at the Bank of England have held rates steady since late last year, as the war in the Middle East pushed up energy prices.

item.title: As India Temporarily Bans Telegram, Which Other Countries Have Restricted the Messaging App?
item.description: Several countries have restricted the messaging app, a valuable platform for dissidents, criminals and extremists. Here’s an overview.
item.dc:creator: John Yoon
item.media:credit: Sasha Maslov for The New York Times

item.title: The Small Investors Behind SpaceX’s Stock Rally
item.description: The company’s blockbuster trading debut is the latest example of how individual investors have become a force in financial markets.
item.dc:creator: Aruni Soni and Joe Rennison
item.media:credit: Andres Kudacki for The New York Times
item.media:description: Since its stock market debut last Friday, SpaceX has been the most bought stock by retail investors each day.

item.title: All Aboard! Brands Jump on the Knicks Bandwagon.
item.description: Companies — including some with no ties to New York or basketball — have posted memes, changed their avatars to Knicks blue and orange, and cheered the team in their captions.
item.dc:creator: Tania Ganguli
